proposed_short: The part of a verb left after you remove the infinitive ending (-are, -ere, -ire); the present-tense endings attach to it.
proposed_long: Every regular Italian verb is built from a stem plus an ending. The stem is what remains once the infinitive ending is stripped off: parlare gives parl-, prendere gives prend-, dormire gives dorm-. The present-tense personal endings (-o, -i, -a/-e, -iamo, -ate/-ete/-ite, -ano/-ono) are then added to that stem. Many irregular verbs are irregular precisely because their stem changes (esco from uscire, vengo from venire) or expands (bev- from bere) before the otherwise-regular endings are added, which is why naming the stem separately from the ending is useful.
rationale: Used throughout the present-formation explanations to separate the invariant part of the verb from the personal ending; the existing glossary has 'stem change' and 'stem expansion' but not the base term 'stem'.

proposed_short: A two-vowel glide pronounced in one syllable, such as the ie of siedo or the uo of muoio.
proposed_long: A diphthong is a sequence of two vowel sounds run together within a single syllable. Several Italian verbs diphthongise their stressed stem vowel in the present: sedere turns e into ie (siedo), morire turns o into uo (muoio), and the modal potere shows uo in puoi and può. The diphthong appears only in the forms where the stress falls on the stem (the singular and the third person plural) and collapses back to a single vowel when the stress shifts to the ending (sediamo, moriamo). Italian grammars call this group the dittongo-mobile (mobile-diphthong) verbs.
rationale: Used in the dittongo-shift explanations; the existing glossary has 'vowel change' but not the specific term for the two-vowel glide that those verbs show.

proposed_short: When a verb's forms come from different, unrelated roots rather than from one stem, as essere's sono / sei / è / siamo.
proposed_long: A paradigm is suppletive when its forms are supplied by historically different roots, so they cannot be derived from a single stem by regular rules. Essere is the clearest Italian case: sono, sei, è, siamo, siete come from more than one Latin source and have to be memorised individually. Suppletion is common cross-linguistically in exactly the highest-frequency verbs (compare English be / am / is / are / was), because frequent forms resist the pressure to regularise. Calling a paradigm suppletive signals to the learner that there is no rule to apply, only forms to learn.
rationale: Used in the essere explanations to flag that there is no derivable pattern; not currently in the glossary.
